paragraphs.body: In the video, “Buying your first rental property,” John Liang analyzes the benefits of investing in real estate. He expresses his point by developing several important ideas. First, Liang explains the tax advantages involved in owning properties. Next, he introduces the benefits of equity. Finally, Liang claims that real estate is in essence the bedrock of your financial growth and stability. Liang’s final point really stood out to me. How could arguably one of the more expensive investment opportunities be the foundation of your investments? I would think that real estate would be a good idea if you already have plenty of savings and it is the cherry on top. This point of his is very perplexing to me and I believe he may be out of touch with his viewers who are trying to get into real estate as opposed to adding to their portfolio.

annotations.text: would think that real estate would be a good idea if you already have plenty of savings and it is the cherry on top.

paragraphs.body: Watching my parents struggle to make ends meet every month was a very difficult thing to witness. At a young age, I didn’t fully understand why I couldn’t get everything that I would ask for after seeing all my friends have the newest clothes, games, or toys. As I started to get older it finally started to make sense to me. I would watch as my dad worked non-stop as a truck driver and was rarely ever able to be home in order to provide for my mom, my two siblings, and myself. He would often miss major events in our lives such as birthdays or our sporting events. As I got into high school, I never held a grudge or got frustrated over him not being around. I understood that in order for us to have some sense of stability, it had to be this way. I learned countless lessons from watching my dad as I grew up such as his work ethic, always having a smile on your face, living in the moment, and that anything was possible. But even with all of these traits that I would come to adopt, there were also aspects I watched that would ultimately hurt the financial wellbeing of our family and my life.

annotations.comment: Great concrete and specific details throughout this paragraph. Nice context.
Nice images, too. They do a great job of developing your ideas.
annotations.context_left: never held a grudge or got frustrated over him not being around.

paragraphs.body: Throughout my life my parents have never been very financially savvy. They would keep all of their money in their bank account and do nothing else with it. It was as simple as ignorance to the world of investing. Because of this, they had never been able to get ahead in life and unfortunately have no plans for retirement. This ignorance to financial literacy would ultimately affect my life as well. As I was in high school and creeping up to the age of independence, I had no clue how to manage money or save money for my own retirement. I would be entering adulthood as blissfully unaware of these factors as my parents had. I don’t blame them for never teaching me as there is no way to teach something you are unaware of, but it was frustrating entering this period of my life where decisions like these could be life changing at my young age. Watching as my parents struggled financially, I did not want my life to end up the same. With this in mind, I made the decision to learn as much as I could about the proper ways to save money and invest so I could live a more stable life. As I look back on this decision, I never would have taken it upon myself to learn these things if it weren’t for my parents’ ineptitude in saving money. With my dad’s exceptional other traits as well as my own willingness to learn how to save money, I had all the keys for success within me. As I was on the verge of graduating high school I had little to no interest in going to college. I never even talked to my parents about the possibility of me going. I felt unprecedented guilt even considering talking to them about it. It was as if I felt like they had wasted enough money on me. These feelings quickly ruled out college as a possibility as there was no way I was willing to take on over a hundred thousand dollars in loans. So, with financial considerations always in mind I had decided to go out on my own and join the Marine Corps. Over my four years in the military, I managed to save up a respectable amount of money and immediately put it to work. I opened a 401k and invested a large portion of my savings in the stock market before I turned 20. As my time in service was coming to an end, I wanted to get ahead and figure out what my new life was going to be. I found a program through the military that allowed me to essentially forgo my last few months in service and apply for an internship. Being from the Pittsburgh area, I applied for an internship in the revenue cycle department at UPMC. Up to this point in my life I had no prior experience with computers, excel, or business in general. All of these were prerequisites for the internship. In my interview for the position, I was in the middle of training exercises for the military. I had to borrow my friend’s car, drive 15 miles to where I could find a drop of service, and joined the interview. Instead of being intimidated as I probably should have been, I took this opportunity that I was given and simply sold myself to them. I may not have had experience in this field and I may not have had a degree that they would be looking for, but what I did have was the desire to learn, to be apart of their world, and I was not going to stop at anything to let that dream be shattered. I showed them something far more important than prerequisite skills they may have been looking for. I showed them my determination and passion that I had to be an asset to their department. I proved to myself that it does not matter where you came from or where you are in life, if you set your mind to something then there are no barriers stopping you from accomplishing your goal.

annotations.text: As I was on the verge of graduating high school I had little to no interest in going to college. I never even talked to my parents about the possibility of me going. I felt unprecedented guilt even considering talking to them about it. It was as if I felt like they had wasted enough money on me. These feelings quickly ruled out college as a possibility as there was no way I was willing to take on over a hundred thousand dollars in loans.
annotations.comment: Your voice is authentic and engaging throughout this essay :)

annotations.context_left: y put it to work. I

paragraphs.body: What I had really been striving for my whole life was real estate. My first real experience with real estate began when my dad mentioned that a friend of his named Joe was looking to sell a rental property he owned at Indiana University of Pennsylvania. As he was aware this was something I had recently been interested in, he gave me Joe’s number and I immediately called him to inquire about the place. We then set up a date to meet at his property and learn more about this experience. Liang states, “Even though the numbers are beautiful and crisp and you’re seeing the dollar signs, you might go in and realize this is not it.” In other words, Liang believes that sometimes you just need to see what you are getting into and touring the property is an essential step in finding a property that is right for you. I found myself making scheduling a tour for this place a top priority just like Liang, as I really wanted to make sure the property was worth the investment in its structure and physical qualities. Owning my own property is the lifestyle that I want to achieve. According to Liang, “Income minus expenses folks, what’s coming in the door, what’s going out the door? If that number is positive, then you’re good to go.” Liang’s point is that in order to know if the property will really be worth investing in, you need to be profiting more than you are spending on it. After spending several hours with Joe and further considering this opportunity, I realized that this important point that Liang made did not seem to be the case for me. As much as I wanted this opportunity to work out, it ultimately was just not meant to be and I will need to continue my search.

annotations.text: I found myself making scheduling a tour for this place a top priority just like

annotations.comment: try an introduction sentence here (the top bun of the quote burger) to bridge your essay ideas to Liang's article and ideas before you include the quote. Right now, the shift from your voice to a different article is abrupt and a bit confusing. :)

paragraphs.body: I have been building my house brick by brick with a strong foundation. I didn’t have the benefit of having the blueprints or the foundation already in place for me. Instead, I grabbed life by the horns and forged my own path. Was it easy? Of course not. Life isn’t meant to be easy or fair. I essentially gambled my life for four years in the military to kickstart my dream of financial stability. I have refused to get complacent and continue to place my bricks day by day. I found a way to break the cycle of impoverished expectations placed upon me unfairly based on my family’s status. All I ever heard from people in similar situations is complaining and thinking that the world is against them. I am a living, breathing example of what can be done to change your position. I never thought of myself as a victim of my situation, but rather a blank slate with the world at my fingertips. This type of mindset has gotten me to places I never thought I would be and I am just getting started.

annotations.text: I have been building my house brick by brick with a strong foundation. I didn’t have the benefit of having the blueprints or the foundation already in place for me.
annotations.comment: Excellent, and surprising, use of metaphor! Nice.
be careful to control the metaphor :)
perhaps the "mixed" metaphor here isn't necessary?

paragraphs.body: The point that Liang was making in his video was that you should think of wealth building like building a house. It happens piece by piece and not all in one swing. Wealth will not build itself for you. You have to roll up your sleeves and be willing to take the time and effort to build it. Many priorities of Liang will also hold to be priorities of mine as I continue on in life. By combining what my dad has taught me in life with what I have learned on my own, there is nothing in this life that I cannot overcome and conquer and so can you.

annotations.comment: Nice job making your essay's point clear. This is a cohesive and engaging essay. Well done.
